checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON against the source PDF page by page. Every heading, list item, and table entry in the JSON has a clear corresponding element in the source document. There are no invented sections or body text. The table captions (e.g., 'General Information') and image alt text are acceptable AI-generated additions for accessibility.

The source document contains empty table rows for 'Email' and 'Phone' and text references to two apps/plans ('NC GIS Contact App', 'PACE plan'). The JSON preserves these text references without adding any invented URLs, phone numbers, or email addresses. No fabricated contact info was found.

All three pages of the source PDF are fully represented in the JSON output. All major sections ('Staff Training & Development', 'External Coordination & Partnerships', 'Documentation & Planning', 'Technology & Infrastructure', 'Data Management & Quality', and 'Emergency Response Tools & Maps') and their respective items are included.

I counted the rows in the 'External Coordination & Partnerships Contacts' table on page 2. Both the source PDF and the JSON (element 17) contain a header row followed by 5 empty data rows. The checkbox list items that were converted into tables (e.g., in 'Documentation & Planning') also accurately reflect the number of items in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The core content of every section is present. While the repetitive header text ('North Carolina Local Government Committee', etc.) and footer metadata (page numbers, 'Last Update') are handled slightly differently on each page or omitted from some semantic blocks, this is considered document furniture and not a significant content omission. The primary checklist items and contact table are 100% present.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text within the JSON is highly faithful to the source material. For example, the list items under 'Emergency Response Tools & Maps' match exactly, including the duplicate entry for 'Create a local road and local facilities map series for offline use' which appears twice in the source document. No wording changes were identified that would alter the meaning for a government employee.

checks.a_analysis: The output correctly handles repeated page headers (North Carolina Local Government Committee, etc.) by including them once per section corresponding to each page. This repetition exists in the source and is not a defect. No unintended content duplication (e.g., repeating list items or paragraphs within a section) was found.

The output maintains a logical heading hierarchy. In Section 1, it moves from H1 to H3 sequentially. Subsequent checklist categories are correctly assigned H2. While the formatting of the page headers varies slightly between sections (e.g., merged H1 in Section 2 vs. H1 and paragraphs in Section 3), this is a normalization choice for accessibility and does not skip levels or create a broken hierarchy.

The contact table in Section 2 (Element 17) does not match the source structure. The source table on page 2 ('External Coordination & Partnerships Contacts') contains a header row followed by 6 empty rows. The converted output for this table contains the header row but only 5 empty data rows, missing one row from the original form.
checks.b_evidence_source: The table under 'External Coordination & Partnerships Contacts' on page 2 contains 6 empty rows for contact entries.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'rows' array for the table in Section 2, element 17, contains only 5 data rows (indices 1 through 5) following the header row.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order in the output logically follows the top-to-bottom flow of the source document. Items such as the page headers, form fields, and checklist sections appear in their intended sequence. Images (logos and QR codes) are placed at the end of their respective sections, which is an acceptable accessibility optimization.

Checklists in the source are converted into either list elements (Section 1) or 2-column key-value tables (Sections 2 and 3). While the conversion type is inconsistent across pages, both methods accurately preserve the meaning and relationship of the checklist items to their selection state (captured as 'false' in tables). No items are merged or split incorrectly.

The output does not contain any link metadata. However, there are no literal URL strings (e.g., ending in .gov, .org) visible in the source text that were missed. The blue/underlined text (e.g., 'NC GIS Contact App', 'PACE plan') indicates hyperlinks in the PDF, but since the raw URLs are not visible as text, their absence in a standard text-to-JSON conversion is typical and does not violate the specific criteria for S7.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully follows the visual organization of the source PDF. The multi-page PDF is converted into a single continuous HTML page, with content from each PDF page appearing in the correct sequence. The checklists in the later sections are represented as tables with 'Field' and 'Value' columns, which is a common and accessible way to render form elements in HTML. While the first two checklist sections are rendered as bulleted lists instead of tables, the information is logically placed and easy to find. Page headers are repeated at the transition points between what were separate PDF pages, which maintains the context of the original document structure.

The contact information table from PDF page 2 ('Name', 'Title', 'Email', 'Phone') is correctly rendered as an HTML table with appropriate headers. The checklist items and form fields (Year, Organization, Contact) are also rendered as structured tables, ensuring they are readable and distinct. There are no signs of overlapping text or broken borders that would impede understanding.

The primary meaningful images from the source PDF, including the 'Hurri Up / LGC' logos and the QR code, are successfully rendered in the HTML output. The logos appear at the start of each section corresponding to a new PDF page, and the QR code is consolidated effectively. There are no broken image icons or missing meaningful visual content.

There is no genuine duplication of body content (paragraphs or data tables) that was not already present in the source PDF (e.g., the repeated map series task on page 3). The repetition of the document header (Logo and Committee name) corresponds to the start of each original PDF page, which is an expected behavior in page-by-page document conversion and does not constitute a defect in content duplication.
